text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 23 August 2022 

Dr Helen Ward  
Northgate Medical Practice  
14 Northgate, Leeds LS2 8ER  

Dear Dr Helen Ward,  

Michael Turner, DOB: 22-11-1983, Hospital No: L928374 NHS No. 6042197751  
Flat 3, 27 Brookfield Road, Leeds, LS6 4ET 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Recurrent seizures on background of prior stable control; deterioration associated with frequent travel and occasional missed doses; mood low; sleep disruption; vitamin D deficiency under treatment; smoker. 

Medication: Levetiracetam 1500mg twice daily.  
Lamotrigine 100mg twice daily.  
Vitamin D3 800 IU daily.  
Omeprazole 20mg once daily.  

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today. He attended alone, arriving directly from a business trip. Over the past eight weeks he has been travelling most weeks, including early flights and irregular mealtimes. He reports increased fatigue, reduced sleep, and acknowledges that he has occasionally missed doses when flights are delayed or luggage is misplaced. He describes feeling more anxious about the unpredictability of events and notes lower mood. 

Seizures: He reports clear worsening compared with earlier this year. Device metrics flag one cluster this week; duration three h. He confirms that this pattern has recurred on a weekly basis recently, with multiple brief events within each cluster, often beginning late evening after days involving travel. He denies prolonged loss of consciousness but describes repetitive episodes of brief unresponsiveness with subsequent confusion, sometimes progressing to whole‑body stiffness. No new injuries, but he has sustained tongue biting twice in the past month. There is no consistent aura. No new triggers other than sleep loss and missed doses. 

Examination: Pulse 74 regular, BP 128/78, weight stable. Neurological examination unremarkable between events; gait normal; no focal deficits elicited today. Skin intact; no head or shoulder bruising. 

Investigations: Home monitor uploads reviewed in clinic corroborate weekly clustering with multiple events per cluster over the last four weeks. Routine bloods three months ago were normal; vitamin D now replete on supplementation. No recent drug interactions identified. 

Assessment: Deteriorating seizure control temporally associated with frequent travel and occasional missed doses. No clear alternative precipitant identified. Risk heightened by weekly clustering and breakthrough events. 

Plan:  
- Emphasised adherence strategies when travelling (carry‑on pill organiser, phone alarms aligned to local time, spare supply in hand luggage).  
- No immediate change to antiepileptic regimen today; will review response to adherence measures within four weeks.  
- Arrange serum levels (lamotrigine trough) and routine bloods in the next 7–10 days.  
- Provide fit note and advised avoiding high‑risk activities until stability returns; discussed DVLA guidance.  
- Book nurse follow‑up in two weeks to review device uploads and adherence log; consultant review in four weeks.  
- Safety: reinforced rescue plan and nocturnal supervision during travel where possible. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states that device metrics flagged one cluster this week and that this pattern has recurred on a weekly basis recently, with multiple brief events within each cluster. Investigations corroborate weekly clustering with multiple events per cluster over the last four weeks. Therefore, the highest-resolution explicit frequency is clusters occurring weekly, with an unspecified number of events per cluster described as 'multiple'. This fits the allowed format '<value1> cluster per <value2> <period>, <value3> per cluster' where value3 is 'multiple' when exact number is not given.
